topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight. My stomach clenched immediately, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. The headline screamed my name, the accompanying photo was stunning, and the accolades were piled high. But the byline? It belonged to a stranger. A celebrated, established voice whose name I vaguely recognized from a few industry magazines. I reread the text, the words I had poured my soul into, now belonging to someone else’s triumph. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I stared at the screen, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to reconcile the reality of the published piece with the phantom feeling of ownership I had cherished. I felt a sudden, sharp desire to tear the screen apart, to erase the evidence that this success wasn't mine.

[story 2]

I walked into the office, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. When Sarah showed me the final printout, the vibrant layout, my breath hitched. It was perfect. And the name on the front page—it wasn't mine. A slow, deliberate movement took over my hands; I smoothed the paper unnecessarily, tracing the unfamiliar signature with a fingertip. A bitter taste flooded my mouth. I watched as Sarah beamed, recounting the reception, her voice ringing with effortless authority. I leaned against the filing cabinet, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, feeling a distinct hollowness bloom in my core. I wanted to shout, to demand recognition, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 3]

The moment the notification popped up, my energy seemed to drain away, leaving me feeling strangely hollowed out. I clicked the link, and there it was: my work, polished, lauded, attributed to another. A strange, cold wave washed over me, chilling the warmth I’d felt moments before. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur beneath the glass. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I picked up my phone, intending to call an editor, but the sheer scale of the achievement felt too vast, too distant. I let the phone slip from my numb fingers, watching it clatter onto the carpet, a small, pathetic sound in the quiet room.
